title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about photography and visual art. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Decisive Moment
content.subtitle: What makes a photograph more than just a picture
content.text:
When Henri Cartier-Bresson described photography as the art of capturing the 'decisive moment' — that fraction of a second in which form, light, and meaning converge — he was articulating something that every serious photographer recognises instinctively even if they lack the language to express it. [31]The decisive moment is not simply about timing; it is about the photographer's ability to perceive, in the chaos of the visible world, a configuration of elements that temporarily achieves the coherence and significance of a composed image.[/31]

Photography's relationship with reality has always been complicated by a deep ambiguity. [32]The camera produces images that carry the authority of mechanical reproduction — the photograph seems to say: this is exactly what was there — while at the same time, every choice the photographer makes (the angle, the frame, the moment of exposure) is an interpretive act that constructs rather than merely records.[/32]

This tension between document and interpretation runs through the history of photography as an art form. [33]Photographers who worked in traditions of social documentary — Walker Evans, Dorothea Lange, Sebastiao Salgado — understood that their images, however powerful as social testimony, were also carefully composed aesthetic objects, and that these two qualities were inseparable rather than contradictory.[/33]

The digital revolution has transformed photography in ways that extend well beyond equipment and technique. [34]The ability to capture hundreds of images in minutes, to review and delete immediately, and to apply post-processing adjustments that alter exposure, colour, and composition means that the relationship between the photographer and the act of seeing has fundamentally changed.[/34] Some photographers welcome this liberation; others argue that it has diminished the discipline that scarcity once imposed.

The abundance of digital images has also altered our relationship with photographic meaning. [35]When photographs were expensive and effortful to produce, each image carried a certain weight of intention. The surfeit of images in the digital age has, arguably, reduced this weight, making individual photographs feel interchangeable and temporary in ways they rarely did before.[/35]

[36]Yet photography continues to produce images of extraordinary power. The camera in the hands of a skilled and attentive photographer remains one of the most effective tools for making visible what is otherwise overlooked — the moment of grace in an ordinary face, the geometry hiding in a city street, the fleeting quality of ordinary light.[/36]

questions.question: What does Cartier-Bresson's concept of the 'decisive moment' really involve?
options: A) The technical skill required to operate a camera at maximum speed.
B) The ability to anticipate events before they occur in order to prepare a shot.
C) The capacity to recognise a brief moment when visual elements combine into meaningful form.
D) The instinct to photograph whatever attracts the eye without conscious reflection.
questions.explanation: The decisive moment is about 'the photographer's ability to perceive a configuration of elements that temporarily achieves the coherence and significance of a composed image.'

questions.question: What ambiguity in photography does the second paragraph describe?
options: A) The uncertainty about whether photographs should be regarded as art or as historical documents.
B) The fact that photographs appear to capture reality objectively while being shaped by the photographer's choices.
C) The difficulty of distinguishing between good and poor photographic technique.
D) The question of whether digital photography is as artistically valid as traditional film photography.
questions.explanation: The camera seems to record exactly 'what was there,' yet every choice (angle, frame, moment) is 'an interpretive act that constructs rather than merely records.'

questions.question: What does the article suggest about the work of social documentary photographers?
options: A) That their primary purpose was always political activism rather than aesthetic achievement.
B) That their images were powerful as documents but weak as composed aesthetic pictures.
C) That they deliberately sacrificed artistic quality in pursuit of greater social impact.
D) That their documentary intent and artistic qualities coexisted rather than conflicted with each other.
questions.explanation: These photographers understood their images were 'powerful as social testimony' and 'carefully composed aesthetic objects,' with 'these two qualities inseparable rather than contradictory.'

questions.question: What fundamental change does the article identify in the digital age of photography?
options: A) The photographer's engagement with the act of seeing has been fundamentally altered by new technology.
B) Photographs can now be taken in conditions of darkness and movement that were impossible before.
C) Professional photographers have largely been replaced by amateurs equipped with powerful smartphones.
D) Post-processing software has made technical skill in taking photographs entirely unnecessary.
questions.explanation: The ability to shoot hundreds of images, review instantly, and adjust in post-processing 'means that the relationship between the photographer and the act of seeing has fundamentally changed.'

questions.question: What effect has the abundance of digital photographs had, according to the article?
options: A) It has made high-quality photography more accessible to people without professional training.
B) It has caused photography exhibitions to become less popular with general audiences.
C) It has made individual photographs seem less significant and more disposable than before.
D) It has encouraged photographers to take more risks with unconventional compositions.
questions.explanation: The 'surfeit of images' has 'reduced this weight, making individual photographs feel interchangeable and temporary in ways they rarely did before.'

questions.question: What does the final paragraph suggest about the continuing potential of photography?
options: A) That digital technology has completely transformed the way photographs communicate meaning.
B) That in skilled hands, photography can reveal overlooked qualities and moments in the visible world.
C) That photographs of ordinary subjects are more artistically valuable than those of dramatic events.
D) That photography is the most powerful of all the visual arts in its ability to move people.
questions.explanation: The camera 'remains one of the most effective tools for making visible what is otherwise overlooked,' such as grace in a face, geometry in a street, and fleeting light.
